_meta.notes: Chapter 1 Lesson 9. Previously taught: kumusta, mabuti, ikaw, magandang, umaga, gabi, po, naman, sige, oo, hindi, opo, salamat, walang anuman, pasensya na, siyempre, pare, mare.

cards.body: Time to talk about people. Tagalog pronouns are simpler than you'd expect.

example.note: Ako can go at the start or end of a sentence.

cards.body: Ka means 'you.' It's the short, casual form of ikaw.

example.note: Both mean 'how are you?' Ka is more common in everyday speech.

cards.body: Siya means 'he,' 'she,' or 'they.' Tagalog doesn't distinguish gender in pronouns.

example.note: One word for everyone. No guessing.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: Tagalog has never had separate words for he and she. Filipino speakers sometimes mix up 'he' and 'she' in English because of this.

cards.source_text: Your friend asks about Maria. Kumusta siya?
cards.explanation: Kumusta siya = How is he/she? Siya refers to Maria here.

cards.body: Ako = I/me. Ka (or ikaw) = you. Siya = he/she/they.

cards.source_text: Someone asks how you are: Kumusta ka?
cards.explanation: Mabuti naman ako = I'm good. Ako = I/me.

cards.prompt: Ask your friend how Jun is doing.

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue. User must get ALL questions right to complete. Cannot finish lesson with outstanding wrong answers.
wrong_answer_tracking.wrong_count_usage: Feed to Gemini system prompt for AI Practice.

alam_mo_ba: Many Filipino speakers accidentally swap 'he' and 'she' in English. It's not a mistake in Tagalog because siya covers both.
Ikaw and ka both mean 'you,' but ikaw is used at the start of a sentence or for emphasis. Ka is used everywhere else.
Tagalog pronouns don't change based on subject or object like English (I vs me). Ako is always ako.
